title: Foundations of Life Insurance
description: A beginner-friendly course that teaches how life insurance works, how to choose the right policy, and how to protect your financial future.

modules.title: Introduction to Life Insurance
modules.objective: Understand the purpose of life insurance and why it is important.
lesson: Life insurance is a contract between you and an insurance company that provides financial support to your loved ones after your death.
Its main purpose is to replace lost income and cover financial responsibilities.
Without life insurance, families may struggle to pay for daily expenses, debts, or long-term goals.
Life insurance is not just for older people — it is most useful when others depend on you financially.
examples: A parent uses life insurance to ensure their children’s education is covered.
A couple uses it to make sure the mortgage can still be paid.
key_takeaways: Life insurance protects your loved ones financially
It replaces income and covers major expenses
It is important if others depend on you

modules.title: Types of Life Insurance
modules.objective: Learn the different types of life insurance and when to use each.
lesson: There are two main categories: term life and permanent life insurance.
Term life insurance provides coverage for a set period and is usually more affordable.
Permanent life insurance lasts your entire life and includes a savings component called cash value.
Different types serve different goals — short-term protection vs long-term financial planning.
examples: A recent graduate may choose term insurance because it is cheaper.
Someone planning inheritance may choose whole life insurance.
key_takeaways: Permanent life = lifelong and builds value
Choose based on your financial goals

quiz.question: Which type of insurance is typically cheaper?

modules.title: Life Insurance Riders
modules.objective: Understand what riders are, how they work, and how to choose the right ones based on real-life situations.
lesson: A rider is an optional add-on to your life insurance policy that provides extra protection or benefits.
Think of riders like customizing your phone with extra features — you only add what you actually need.
While a basic policy covers death, riders can protect you in situations like disability, illness, or unexpected events.
One common rider is the Waiver of Premium Rider. If you become disabled and cannot work, this rider allows you to keep your policy without paying premiums.
Another is the Accidental Death Benefit Rider, which increases the payout if death occurs due to an accident.
The Guaranteed Insurability Rider allows you to increase your coverage later without taking another medical exam. This is useful if your income grows or you start a family.
A Long-Term Care Rider lets you use part of your life insurance benefit while you are still alive to pay for healthcare or assisted living costs.
Not all riders are necessary. Choosing the right ones depends on your lifestyle, health risks, and financial goals.
Adding too many riders can make your policy expensive, so it’s important to balance cost and benefit.
examples: If you work a physically demanding job, a Waiver of Premium Rider can protect you if you become injured.
If you are young and healthy now, a Guaranteed Insurability Rider lets you increase coverage later without worrying about future health issues.
If you are concerned about medical costs later in life, a Long-Term Care Rider can help cover those expenses.
key_takeaways: Riders customize your life insurance policy
They provide protection beyond basic death coverage
Choose riders based on your personal risks and future plans
More riders = higher cost, so choose wisely

quiz.question: What is the main purpose of a life insurance rider?
options: To add extra benefits or protection
quiz.answer: To add extra benefits or protection

quiz.question: Which rider helps if you become unable to work?
Why might someone choose a Guaranteed Insurability Rider?
options: To increase coverage later without a medical exam
quiz.answer: To increase coverage later without a medical exam

modules.title: Calculating Life Insurance Needs
modules.objective: Learn how to calculate how much coverage you need.
lesson: Start by listing your financial obligations such as debts, rent, and education costs.
Estimate how much income your family would need if you were no longer there.
Subtract any savings or existing insurance coverage.
The remaining amount is your coverage gap.
Choosing the right amount ensures your family is protected without overpaying.
examples: If your family needs $600,000 and you already have $200,000, you need $400,000 coverage.

quiz.question: What should you subtract when calculating needs?

modules.title: Policy Components
modules.objective: Understand the key parts of a life insurance policy.
lesson: The premium is the amount you pay regularly for coverage.
The death benefit is the amount paid to your beneficiaries.
Cash value is a savings feature found in some policies.
Understanding these components helps you choose the right policy.
examples: A $300,000 policy means your beneficiaries receive $300,000.

modules.title: Buying and Managing Life Insurance
modules.objective: Learn how to choose and maintain your policy.
lesson: Compare insurance companies based on reputation and financial strength.
Always read and understand your policy before purchasing.
Update your beneficiaries when life changes occur.
Review your policy regularly to make sure it still meets your needs.
examples: After having a child, you may increase your coverage.
After paying off debt, you may reduce coverage.
key_takeaways: Update it as your life changes

quiz.question: When should you review your policy?

modules.title: Life Insurance Strategies
modules.objective: Understand how to use life insurance strategically.
lesson: Laddering involves buying multiple term policies with different lengths.
This helps match coverage to changing financial needs.
Permanent insurance can also be used for long-term planning and wealth transfer.
The key is to balance affordability with protection.
examples: A person may have a 10-year and 20-year policy at the same time.

quiz.question: What is laddering?
options: Using multiple policies with different terms
quiz.answer: Using multiple policies with different terms

modules.title: Case Studies & Practice
modules.objective: Apply what you’ve learned to real-life scenarios.
lesson: Understanding concepts is important, but applying them is what builds confidence.
In real life, every person’s situation is different.
You must consider income, family needs, and long-term goals.
examples: A young professional may choose term insurance with low premiums.
A parent may choose higher coverage with added riders.
key_takeaways: Apply concepts based on real needs
